result:
The first time I noticed the quiet, I was standing in the produce aisle of a 24-hour grocery store at 2:17 a.m., fingering a bruise on an avocado the color of old pennies. Fluorescent lights hummed overhead like a hive that had forgotten how to die. I had come for bananas—three, slightly green, because my daughter liked them firm enough to thud when she dropped them on the kitchen tiles. Instead I found myself listening to the automatic mister sigh across the romaine, watching beads of water roll down the plastic curtain like tears that had memorized their path. Somewhere behind the onion pyramid a radio played a song I once slow-danced to in a stranger’s kitchen, barefoot on cracked linoleum, pretending the future was a recipe I could still taste. I couldn’t remember the boy’s face, only the way his collar smelled of dish soap and clove cigarettes, and how the burner clicked four times before the flame caught. I left the avocado in the bin with its darker twin and walked out without buying anything at all. Outside, the parking lot was a lake of sodium light. My breath fogged the windshield just enough to blur the moon, and for a moment the sky looked like a thumb-smudged charcoal drawing I might have made in eighth grade, when I still believed art was a form of rescue.

I used to think growing older would feel like climbing a staircase—each step deliberate, the banister smooth under my palm, the next floor suddenly visible. Instead it feels like wading into the ocean at dusk: one cold shock, then the numb surrender, the sand eroding underfoot so gradually you don’t notice you’re farther out than you meant to be until a wave slaps your chest and the lights on shore are smaller than birthday candles. I am forty-one, an age that refuses to pose for the camera. It ducks behind its own shoulder, muttering maybe later. My daughter believes I know the names of all the stars; I have Googled them in secret while she sleeps, whispering “Betelgeuse, Rigel, Saiph” like a spell against the day she realizes I am mostly echo. At night I walk the hallway between her room and the bathroom counting heartbeats instead of sheep, measuring the distance between the next breath and the last catastrophe I didn’t see coming. Some evenings I open the medicine cabinet just to stare at the amber bottles, reading the fine print as if it were travel literature: may cause drowsiness, may cause dreams of falling, may cause you to remember the exact timbre of your mother’s voice the day she taught you to tie your shoes double-knotted so you wouldn’t trip. I close the door, switch off the light, and let the dark rearrange my face.

Memory keeps its own accounting. For instance: the summer I was ten, a neighbor boy and I built a city for caterpillars in a cracked flowerpot. We crowned a monarch “mayor,” christened highways with the names of candy bars, and held funerals for the ones who curled into question marks before they could become anything else. One afternoon he kissed me beside the garage, quick as a burglar, then sprinted away leaving only the echo of cicadas and the smell of cut grass. I stood there touching my mouth the way you press a bruise to see if it still hurts, unsure whether the sting was shame or prophecy. Three weeks later his family moved to Arizona. I kept the flowerpot until the first frost, long after the survivors had wandered off, leaving behind a silk hammock of empty cocoons that looked like tiny paper lanterns no one remembered to light. Sometimes I wonder if he ever thinks of that day, or if it drifted out of him the way loose change slips through a pocket hole, one unnoticed jingle at a time.

When my father died, the hospital gave us a plastic bag containing his watch, his wallet, and the paperback he had been reading—an espionage thriller whose final forty pages would remain forever dog-eared at the moment the hero leapt from a moving train. I drove home with that bag on the passenger seat, obeying every speed limit as if the rules of grief were posted on white metal signs. For weeks the book sat on my desk like a paused life. I never opened it. Instead I listened to the sound of my own clock, the way it swallowed seconds whole, indifferent to plot. One night I dreamed I was the one on the train, pages flapping like desperate birds around my head, my father’s voice reading the narration: He realized mid-air that the bridge was farther than he calculated, but by then the choice had already made itself. I woke gasping, fingers gripping the mattress edge, convinced I had landed somewhere soft yet unfamiliar.

I read once that the human body replaces most of its cells every seven years, which means I am already a anthology of former selves, each edition slightly edited for clarity. The girl who swallowed a watermelon seed and feared a vine would grow out of her ears—gone, though sometimes I still feel the phantom rustle of leaves. The teenager who carved someone’s initials into her thigh with a safety pin—gone, scar faint as a rumor. The woman who stood at the altar believing marriage was a kind of weather you could dress for—gone, her dress preserved in an attic box like evidence from a crime scene no one intends to solve. What persists is not the cells themselves but the stories they whisper to the newcomers: you were loved, you were foolish, you survived. The body is a palimpsest, every inflammation a footnote, every stretch mark a marginalia that says here the sky expanded without warning.

Last Tuesday I took the day off work and rode the city bus to the terminal point, a shopping mall built on drained marshland where red-winged blackbirds once balanced on cattails like tiny tightrope walkers. I walked the food court inhaling reheated grease and cinnamon, eavesdropping on teenagers who spoke in a dialect half meme, half prayer. Their laughter cracked the air like knuckles, and I felt the familiar ache of overhearing a future that no longer has my name in the credits. I bought a lemonade that tasted faintly of bleach and sat beside the indoor fountain, watching coins settle on the blue tiles—wish sediment, layer upon layer. A small girl in sparkly sneakers approached, clutching a penny like a secret. She screwed her eyes shut, whispered something I couldn’t catch, and flipped the coin. It landed tails-up, but she grinned anyway, already pivoting toward whatever miracle she had scheduled next. I thought about telling her that wishes are just stories we haven’t learned to doubt yet, but I didn’t. Instead I gave her the rest of my lemonade. She took it with the solemnity of a knight accepting a quest, then ran off, sloshing tribute across the tiles. I stayed until the security guard started locking gates, until the fountain shut off with a mechanical sigh, until the last coin looked less like hope and more like a tiny copper moon sinking in an artificial sky.

Driving home, I listened to a radio call-in show about near-death experiences. A trucker described hovering above his overturned rig, watching paramedics pound on his chest while he felt nothing but warmth, the way you slip into a bath after the day has scraped you raw. The host asked if he had seen a tunnel, a light, departed relatives beckoning. The trucker laughed, a sound like tires retreading gravel. “Nah, man. It was just the rest area on I-80 I always miss because I’m running late. This time I finally got to stop.” I changed the station before the host could respond, but the sentence stayed with me, mile after mile, until I turned onto my street where the maple out front was dropping leaves shaped like tiny failures. I parked, sat in the dark, and imagined that rest area: vending machines humming lullabies, fluorescent picnic tables clean enough to write your name in condensation, and somewhere beyond the hedge the faint smell of diesel mixed with lilacs blooming where no one planted them anymore. I thought maybe that’s all eternity is—a moment generous enough to let you catch your breath before you climb back into the cab of your ongoing life, keys still warm in your hand, the road unrolling like a receipt you’ll never finish reading.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
